coredata.dc:title: Wafer-level flexible carbon-based film for fluorescent display and optical information storage
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Shengguo
Bi, Kaixi
Hou, Liuyu
Guo, Miaoli
Zhuang, Yan
Zhang, Shuai
Li, Qiannan
Han, Shuqi
Liu, Longhao
Mei, Linyu
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent materials have garnered significant interest in optical display and information storage fields for their vibrant and distinct color presentation. Extensive research has been conducted on carbon-based fluorescent materials owing to their environmental sustainability, versatility, and adjustable optical properties. However, it is difficult to integrate high-resolution fluorescent structure on various wafer-level substrate for abundant optical display and information storage. This study successfully fabricated wafer-level carbon-based fluorescent flexible film with ∼100 μm thick using a standardized “lithography + annealing” process. The sample demonstrates high fluorescence intensity with a peak at 603 nm and fluorescence lifetime of 0.3517 ns. When excited by light of 365 nm, 485 nm, and 525 nm wavelengths, the film displays yellow, green, and red, showcasing exceptional color tuning capability. This flexible film is portable and can be readily integrated with various substrates, offering significant technical and theoretical support for advancing carbon-based optical display and information storage technologies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon-based material
Wafer-level and high-resolution films
Fluorescent display
Optical information storage

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and characterization of internal elements
Wafer-level flexible manufacturing of carbon-based fluorescent arrays

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52275577 and Grant No. 52205609); Key Research and Development Project Key Program of Shanxi Province, China (Grant No. 202102040201007); General project of Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (Grant No. 20210302123056 and Grant No. 202203021223005); Shanxi Provincial Postgraduate Scientific Research Innovation Project (Grant No. 2023SJ195 and Grant No. 2023KY597); The 19th graduate science and technology project of North University of China (Grant No. 20231906).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Fluorescence images at 365 nm excitation wavelength after different temperature treatments. (b) Fluorescence images at three different excitation wavelengths: 365 nm, 485 nm, and 525 nm of samples treated at 400°C.

(a-b) SEM images of SU-8 micropillar structure in X-Y plane after annealing treatment. (c-d) SEM images of SU-8 micropillar structure in X-Z plane after annealing treatment. (e) AFM images of the surface of SU-8 micropillar structure after annealing treatment.

(a) Raman spectra after treatment at different annealing temperatures. (b) FTIR spectra after treatment at different annealing temperatures. (c) XRD diffraction patterns after treatment at different annealing temperatures.
(a–c) XPS full spectrum and detailed spectra of SU-8 before annealing. (d–f) XPS full spectrum and detailed spectra of SU-8 after annealing at 400°C.

(a) Fluorescence intensity of samples treated with different annealing temperatures. (b) Excitation and emission spectra of samples treated at 400°C. (c) Fluorescence lifetime maps of SU-8 and samples treated at 400°C at 375 nm excitation wavelength.
(a–e) UV–Vis spectrum of SU-8 after treatment at 100°C–500°C. (f–j) Optical band gap of SU-8 after 100°C–500°C treatment.
Physical diagram of flexible carbon-based fluorescent arrays on a wafer scale, and fluorescence images at different wavelength bands.
Fluorescent display patterns under illumination from light sources of different wavelengths.
(a) Schematic diagram of carbon-based fluorescent information encryption. (b) Schematic diagram of information extraction.

head.ce:title: Wafer-level flexible carbon-based film for fluorescent display and optical information storage

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical Engineering, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechanical Engineering, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Science and Technology on Electronic Test and Measurement Laboratory, School of Instrument and Electronics, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Science and Technology on Electronic Test and Measurement Laboratory

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Science and Technology on Electronic Test and Measurement Laboratory, School of Instrument and Electronics, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Taiyuan Heavy Machinery Group Co., Ltd., Taiyuan 030024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Taiyuan Heavy Machinery Group Co., Ltd.

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Taiyuan Heavy Machinery Group Co., Ltd., Taiyuan 030024, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent materials have garnered significant interest in optical display and information storage fields for their vibrant and distinct color presentation. Extensive research has been conducted on carbon-based fluorescent materials owing to their environmental sustainability, versatility, and adjustable optical properties. However, it is difficult to integrate high-resolution fluorescent structure on various wafer-level substrate for abundant optical display and information storage. This study successfully fabricated wafer-level carbon-based fluorescent flexible film with ∼100 μm thick using a standardized “lithography + annealing” process. The sample demonstrates high fluorescence intensity with a peak at 603 nm and fluorescence lifetime of 0.3517 ns. When excited by light of 365 nm, 485 nm, and 525 nm wavelengths, the film displays yellow, green, and red, showcasing exceptional color tuning capability. This flexible film is portable and can be readily integrated with various substrates, offering significant technical and theoretical support for advancing carbon-based optical display and information storage technologies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid development of information science and technology, the demand for information storage materials with high storage capacity and high security performance has become increasingly urgent [1–7]. In decades, information storage materials have also evolved from bulk materials to thin and ultrathin layers of microstructured materials. They are also evolving toward optoelectronic information, such as functionally integrated chips, high-resolution microstructure fluorescence, and flexible optical displays [8–14]. Fluorescent materials are considered to be ideal materials for displays and information storage due to their intuitive visualization properties, rich color choices, and facile fabrication [15–19]. Among various fluorescent materials, carbon-based materials (CBMs) stand out for their application potential in anti-counterfeiting encryption, fluorescent displays, and optical information storage, owing to their superior optical properties, precise size control, and low toxicity [20–24]. Currently, research on the high-resolution and reliable preparation of CBMs microstructures and their optical properties has become a hot field.

The synthesis methods of carbon-based materials are mainly divided into two approaches: “top-down” and “bottom-up”. The “top-down” method prepares micro and nanostructures by cutting or decomposing bulk carbon materials, with common techniques including electrochemical cutting and laser ablation. This approach is not only convenient and capable of precisely controlling dimensions but also preserves the morphological structure of the raw materials [25–28]. For example, Shiva et al. synthesized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNT) nanocomposites for dopamine detection using electrochemical methods. The sensor exhibited excellent electrocatalytic activity and sensitivity with a dynamic linear range of 0.25 to 250 μM and a detection limit of 95 nM [29]. Kang et al. synthesized sulfur-containing graphene quantum dots (S-GQDs) by a simple one-step pulsed laser ablation (PLAL) method. It is shown that S-GQDs exhibit a significant improvement in optical properties with a quantum yield (QY) of 3.89% compared to undoped graphene quantum dots. This method not only simplifies the synthesis process, but also overcomes the shortcomings of traditional chemical doping methods, thus laying the foundation for further applications of S-GQDs [30]. On the other hand, the “bottom-up” method involves the pyrolysis or carbonization of organic small molecules, such as microwave-assisted and hydrothermal methods, which are simple, fast, and highly reproducible [31–36]. Zhai et al. synthesized highly luminescent carbon nanodots (CDs) from citric acid and amine molecules based on microwave-assisted pyrolysis, achieving quantum yields up to 30.2%. Meanwhile, the dual roles of primary amine molecules in the preparation process were explored, including as N-doped precursors and surface passivators, which greatly enhanced the fluorescence properties of the CDs [37]. Dong et al. prepared GQDs through direct pyrolysis of citric acid. It was shown experimentally that GQDs are enriched with small sp2 clusters of uniform size and good surface passivation, and thus exhibit strong and excitation-independent photoluminescence (PL) activity, whereas GO contains inhomogeneous sp2 clusters, resulting in weak and excitation-wavelength dependent PL activity [38].

Although these methods have made important advances in the synthesis of carbon-based materials, there are still fewer studies combining “top-down” and “bottom-up” fabrication methods, which limits the development of carbon-based materials in the field of optical integration. As a “top-down” fabrication method, UV lithography has attracted much attention from researchers for its high-resolution and micro-nanoscale patterning capabilities. The technology has high positioning and alignment accuracy. It can complete the fabrication of a large number of patterns quickly and accurately [39–41]. “Bottom-up” annealing technology significantly improves and optimizes material properties by precisely controlling the heating and cooling processes [42,43]. The combination of these two technologies not only overcomes the limitations of carbon-based materials in optically integrated manufacturing, but also opens up new avenues for their application in a wide range of fields.

SU-8, an aromatic hydrocarbon compound, offers significant advantages in the study of carbon-based fluorescent materials. These properties include a rich source of carbon, structural diversity, and other beneficial characteristics. In our study, we have successfully fabricated carbon-based micro-nano fluorescent array by treating the SU-8 film spin-coated on a silicon substrate using the “lithography + annealing” technique. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) measurements show that the surface roughness of these micro-nanostructures is less than 1 nm. Through detailed analysis of Raman spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction (XRD), and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), the results indicate that high temperature annealing facilitates the conversion of SU-8 to graphitized carbon. We also tested the effect of different annealing temperatures on the fluorescence intensity of the samples and found that the samples annealed at 400°C exhibited the strongest fluorescence intensity and narrowest half-peak width. Using an inverted fluorescence microscope, under the irradiation of three different wavelengths of excitation light, only the sample treated at 400°C showed three different fluorescence colors, namely yellow, green, and red. In addition, by using polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) as a flexible substrate, we realized wafer-level flexible fabrication of carbon-based fluorescent arrays, which opens up new avenues for flexible optoelectronic devices. Finally, complex structures containing letters, Chinese characters, and 2D codes were precisely prepared using UV lithography. Their potential application in display and information security storage technology was demonstrated.

SU-8 2025 photoresist was purchased from Suzhou Advantech, which is a negative epoxy resin-based near-ultraviolet photoresist with high-resolution UV lithography capability. Polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) was purchased from Suzhou Research Material, which is an inexpensive, safe, and non-toxic polymer that is an ideal material for this study due to its excellent light transmission, high chemical inertness, and low Young's modulus for high-quality elastomers. 4-inch monocrystalline silicon wafers and polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) were purchased from Suzhou Research Material, Inc. Anhydrous ethanol (C2H6O, 99%) and acetone (C3H6O, 98%) were purchased from Macklin (Shanghai, China). Deionized water was used for all preparations. All materials were used in their original state.

In the study, high-resolution micro-nano structures were fabricated using an EVG610 UV photolithography tool, which can achieve a lithographic resolution of less than 2 μm. The specific fabrication steps were as follows: SU-8 photoresist was deposited on a silicon wafer of ∼500 μm thickness at a speed of 5000 rpm to form a film of ∼18 μm thickness. The film was baked at 95°C for 10 min and then exposed under UV lithography with a UV dose of 160 mJ/cm2. After exposure, the samples were baked at 95°C for 10 min. The samples were then developed in PGEMA solution for 15 min to form the micro-nano structures. Finally, the samples were rinsed with deionized water and the water spots on the sample surface were blown dry using a nitrogen gas gun. The whole process is shown in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and characterization of internal elements
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, a JSM-7900F Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) was used to characterize the surface and cross-section of the samples at the micro and nano levels, thus obtaining high-resolution images of the planar structure of the samples. In addition, an MFP-3D Origint + Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) with a scanning area of 90 μm × 90 μm and nanometer resolution was used to evaluate the surface roughness and homogeneity of the samples. Raman spectroscopy tests, performed with a WITec alpha 300R confocal Raman imaging system, allowed detailed characterization of the internal elements and defects of the samples. The changes in functional groups within the samples before and after annealing were characterized using a Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) analyzer. An Ultima IV series X-ray diffractometer was also used to characterize the internal crystalline phase changes of the samples before and after annealing. The excitation spectra, emission spectra, and fluorescence lifetimes of the samples were characterized using a fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh FS5). An inverted fluorescence microscope with three different wavelengths was used to characterize the optical properties of the samples and to monitor the changes in the optical properties of the samples.

In this study, carbon-based fluorescent micropillar arrays were successfully integrated onto a silicon substrate by a “lithography + annealing” process. By using an inverted fluorescence microscope, we characterized the fluorescence of the samples annealed at different temperatures ranging from 100°C to 500°C. The samples were found to be fluorescent under the excitation light of 365 nm. It was found that the fluorescent color of the samples varied with the annealing temperature under the excitation light of 365 nm, and the fluorescent color of the samples was most obvious after the annealing treatment at 400°C (Fig. 2 a). Based on this important finding, we conducted an in-depth study of the sample after the 400°C treatment. The results showed that the sample exhibited yellow, green, and red fluorescence under the irradiation of 365 nm, 485 nm, and 525 nm excitation light, respectively, revealing its unique fluorescence characteristics (Fig. 2b). The reasons for this phenomenon are as follows: (1) Electronic energy levels and excited states. Fluorescence occurs when the sample absorbs light energy, causing electrons to transition from the ground state to an excited state, and then emit light energy as they return to a lower excited state or the ground state. When excited by light of different wavelengths, electrons may transition to different excited energy states. Different excited states may lead to the emission of light of different energies as electrons return to the ground state, producing fluorescence of different colors. (2) Molecular structure. The molecular structure of fluorescent substances determines their absorption and emission characteristics of light. Under the excitation light of different wavelengths, the internal electronic transitions and energy level relaxation processes in molecules may differ, thereby resulting in the emission of fluorescence of different colors.

The variation and integrity of the overall structural morphology of the samples in the X-Y and X-Z planes were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). As shown in Fig. 3 (a-d), the observation results show that the topography of the whole array has high integrity and high spatial resolution. After annealing, the sample morphology is in the shape of a round table with a collapsed center, in which the upper feature size is about 19.5 μm and the lower feature size is about 29.6 μm. This change in the morphology of the micropillar structure is mainly due to the pyrolysis reaction of the SU-8 negative photoresist during the high-temperature annealing process and the volatilization of part of the material [44,45]. AFM was used to examine the longitudinal roughness of the sample surface, and the test results are shown in Fig. 3(e). The average variation of the longitudinal dimension was found to be less than 1 nm. This indicates that the samples treated with high temperature annealing not only have low surface roughness, but also have higher integrity.

We performed Raman characterization of the photolithographed SU-8 samples as well as the samples treated at different annealing temperatures and analyzed the Raman signal changes in detail, the results are shown in Fig. 4 (a) [46]. In the Raman spectrum of the SU-8 samples after lithography, three characteristic peaks were observed at ∼810 cm−1, ∼941 cm−1 – ∼983 cm−1, and ∼1180 cm−1, which mainly belong to the epoxy groups, corresponding to the chemical structure of SU-8 [47,48]. When the annealing temperature reaches 200°C, we find that the scattering peaks in the interval ∼941 cm−1 - ∼983 cm−1 disappear. This phenomenon may indicate the pyrolysis reaction within the SU-8 material at this temperature, which leads to the breaking of the internal C-O-C bond and affects the chemical composition of the material. Notably, in the Raman spectrum at 400°C annealing temperature, we observed the presence of D and G peaks for the first time, with an ID/IG ratio of less than 1. This suggests that at an annealing temperature of 400°C, SU-8 may have transformed into a graphitic carbon material with defects [49,50]. The appearance of D and G peaks is usually associated with structural defects and the degree of graphitization in graphene and other carbon materials. This result indicates that SU-8 has undergone significant changes in its structure and chemical composition after high-temperature treatment, forming a carbon-based material with a specific molecular structure. Surface chemical bonding and molecular state may affect the fluorescence effect of the samples by changing the electronic structure and energy level distribution of the material.

The results of the FTIR analysis are shown in Fig. 4(b) [51–53], the characteristic peaks at ∼1463 cm−1, ∼1499 cm−1 and ∼1606 cm−1 confirm the presence of an aromatic ring in the SU-8 monomer molecule, while the peak at ∼912 cm−1 marks the epoxy group in SU-8. The peak at ∼2963 cm−1 represents the methyl C-H vibration on the benzene ring, and the peaks at ∼2866 cm−1 and ∼2923 cm−1 are then attributed to the C-H stretching vibration of propylene oxide. The peak at ∼912 cm−1, as well as the characteristic peak corresponding to the aromatic ring in the SU-8 molecular skeleton, showed a gradual weakening trend with the heat treatment process. This suggests that the epoxy groups may undergo crosslinking or pyrolysis reactions during heat treatment, resulting in ring opening of the epoxy groups. In addition, the FTIR spectra of the samples after heat treatment at 400°C were similar to those of the graphene samples, showing absorption peaks at ∼826 cm−1, ∼1170 cm−1, ∼1460 cm−1 and ∼1605 cm−1, corresponding to the vibrations of C-O-C, C-O, C-OH, and CC, respectively. This suggests that the sample can be transformed into a graphene-like material at a treatment temperature of 400°C, which is consistent with the results of the Raman spectroscopy analysis.

The XRD diffraction pattern are shown in Fig. 4(c), we notice that both the original SU-8 samples and those annealed at temperatures from 100°C to 300°C exhibit broad diffraction peaks, accompanied by a slight right shift of the peak position. This broad diffraction peak indicates the benzene ring structure of SU-8 and its relatively low crystallinity. According to Bragg's law d
= λ / ( 2 sin θ ) , as the thermal treatment temperature increases, the internal benzene rings may undergo thermal decomposition reactions leading to cleavage, causing changes in the interplanar spacing and thereby a slight right shift in the diffraction peak position. Diffraction peaks typical of graphitized materials were observed in samples annealed at 400°C and 500°C (−002). An orderly arrangement of carbon layers is indicated, but the broadening of the diffraction peaks reflects a decrease in crystallinity in the stacking direction. Therefore, combining the results of Raman spectroscopy, we infer that 400°C is the key turning point for the transition of SU-8 to amorphous graphitic carbon material.

The X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) results of the samples after heat treatment at SU-8 and 400°C are shown in Fig. 5 . By comparative analysis of the fine spectrum of the carbon element, we found that the C-O bond located at 286.5 eV disappeared after annealing, and the content of the C-C bond located at 284.5 eV increased from 64.52% to 86.28%. Comparative analysis of the fine spectrum of the oxygen element also shows that the CO bond located at 531.5 eV disappears after annealing, and the C-O bond content located at 532.8 eV increases. These results indicate that during the high-temperature heating process, pyrolysis and cross-linking reactions occur in SU-8, resulting in the cleavage of C-O and CO bonds and the formation of more C-C bonds. It also indicates that SU-8 undergoes decomposition and reorganization to become a graphitized carbon material, and this result is also consistent with previous characterization results.

The conjugated π system inside the carbon-based materials and the surface chemical groups are considered to be the main reason for their photoluminescence. Samples with different degrees of carbonization and different surface states can be obtained by varying different annealing temperatures. For the conjugated π-system, it is the most crucial factor affecting fluorescence. When the material is excited by an external light source, the electrons in a stable state within will absorb energy from the outside and transition to an excited state. Since the energy required for transitions in the conjugated π-system is the smallest among all transitions, during fluorescence, the π→π* transition is the primary mode of transition. After the transition is completed, electrons in an unstable state need to release excess energy to return to a stable state. The released energy will be radiated out in the form of photons, generating fluorescence. The higher the carbonation degree, the easier it is to obtain large conjugated π-systems and surface defect states, which have an important effect on the electron energy level jumping efficiency and further on the fluorescence intensity. The different conjugated π-systems in different degrees of carbonation lead to different electron energy level jumping efficiencies and thus to different fluorescence intensities [54–56]. The carbonized SU-8 mainly consists of an amorphous carbon matrix containing graphite fragment or graphite sheet stacking regions. Its fluorescence is mainly due to the π→π* transition in sp2 hybridized carbon structures. Even in carbon structures larger than 20 μm, specific electronic states and structural features can lead to significant fluorescence properties.

The fluorescence intensity of the samples after different temperature treatments was further tested and the results are shown in Fig. 6 (a). The highest fluorescence intensity was found for the sample after annealing treatment at 400°C. The fluorescence phenomenon observed in carbon-based materials can be attributed to two primary factors: the presence of an internal conjugated π system and the existence of surface defect states. An increase in the degree of carbonization typically facilitates the formation of larger conjugated π systems and a greater number of surface defect states, which exert a considerable influence on the efficiency of electron-level transitions and, subsequently, the intensity of fluorescence.

The Raman spectroscopy analysis of the sample subjected to treatment at 400°C revealed the presence of characteristic peaks associated with carbon-based materials, namely the D and G peaks. Furthermore, the ID/IG ratio was determined to be less than 1, which suggests that the treatment at this temperature resulted in the formation of a defective carbon-based material. Furthermore, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) characterization results corroborate the transformation of the material into a carbon-based material, as evidenced by the appearance of the (−002) diffraction peak characteristic of carbon-based materials in the diffraction pattern of the sample after treatment at 400°C. In contrast, the Raman and XRD spectra of the samples treated at temperatures below 400°C did not display the characteristic peaks of carbon-based materials, indicating that these samples had not yet undergone conversion to carbon-based materials. Consequently, the fluorescence intensity was significantly reduced in comparison to the samples treated at 400°C.

The excitation and emission spectra of the sample at 400°C were also measured, as shown in Fig. 6(b). It can be seen that the excitation wavelength is ∼375 nm and the emission wavelength is ∼603 nm, which have a large Stokes shift. The large Stokes shift helps to reduce the overlap between the excitation light and the fluorescence, which reduces the self-absorption phenomenon, which is favorable for improving the fluorescence stability and efficiency. The large Stokes shift provides better spectral selectivity, which is important for studying the detection and imaging of specific substances in complex biological systems or multi-component systems.

The fluorescence lifetimes of the SU-8 and 400°C treated samples were tested at an excitation wavelength of 375 nm as shown in Fig. 6(c), and the fluorescence lifetimes were 1.0744 ns and 0.3517 ns. The fluorescence decay of SU-8 can be described by the following equation: The fluorescence intensity at time t, I(t), can be expressed as a function of two exponential decay constants, A and B, and two fluorescence lifetimes,
τ 1
and
τ 2
:
I
t
= A
e
- t /
τ 1
+ B
e
- t /
τ 2
. The equation for the fluorescence decay of samples treated at 400℃ is thus given by
I
t
=
I 0
e
- t / τ
, where I(t) is the fluorescence intensity at time t, I0 is the initial fluorescence intensity, and τ is the fluorescence lifetime, which indicates the time for the fluorescence intensity to decay to
1 / e
. Heat treatment significantly shortened the fluorescence lifetime of the samples, a phenomenon that may be facilitated by heat-induced defect formation or non-radiative bonding processes that in turn shorten the fluorescence lifetime. This phenomenon is important for understanding the photophysical properties of materials and their applications in optics.

Following the application of heat treatments at varying temperatures between 100°C and 500°C, ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis) tests were conducted on the heat-treated SU-8 samples. The specific characterization results are presented in Fig. 7 (a-e). Following the application of heat treatments at temperatures of 100°C, 200°C, and 300°C, the samples exhibited absorption peaks at 270 nm and 320 nm. The former peak was attributed to the π→π* transition of the C=C bond, while the latter was attributed to the n→π* transition of the C=O bond. The positions of the absorption peaks remained largely unchanged at these three temperatures, indicating that treatment temperatures below 300°C have a relatively minimal impact on the internal structure of the samples. However, following the treatment at 400°C, a red shift was observed for the π→π* peaks, while the n→π* peaks displayed notable broadening. This suggests that the material initiated a cross-linking reaction and that a portion of the carbon structure underwent a gradual transformation. As the temperature increased, a change in the electronic structure was observed, resulting in the formation of new compounds. Following the treatment at 500°C, the π→π* peaks exhibited a further red shift and broadening, while the n→π* peaks were no longer discernible. These observations suggest that a substantial carbonization reaction may have occurred within the sample at 500°C, resulting in the cleavage of C=O bonds and the formation of additional carbon structures.

Using UV–Vis spectroscopy, we plotted the optical band gap of the samples after treatment at different temperatures, as shown in Fig. 7(f-j). We observed that the optical band gap gradually decreases with increasing temperature. The reason for this phenomenon is that as the annealing temperature increases, the epoxy groups in SU-8 undergo further crosslinking reactions, resulting in a denser and more stable three-dimensional network structure. As the degree of crosslinking increases, the electronic structure of the material changes. A higher degree of crosslinking can introduce more defect states or form a more tightly bound conjugated π system, thereby reducing the optical band gap of the material. Specifically, the decrease in energy of the π→π* transition results in a red shift in the absorption spectrum, which ultimately reduces the optical band gap. In addition, during high temperature annealing, SU-8 partially carbonizes to form carbon-rich structures, which generally have a narrower band gap, further reducing the overall optical band gap of the material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Wafer-level flexible manufacturing of carbon-based fluorescent arrays
ce:section.ce:para: As the diverse demands for carbon-based fluorescent arrays in various application scenarios continue to grow, especially the increasing need for flexible samples, traditional rigid substrates can no longer meet the diversified needs of current applications [57]. Therefore, our research focuses on the manufacturing of flexible samples. Considering the requirements of ultraviolet lithography processes for micro and nanostructures, the selected flexible substrates must possess high flexibility, chemical stability, and ease of preparation. PDMS is considered the ideal substrate due to its excellent chemical stability, thermal stability, low temperature flexibility and full transparency. In the manufacturing process, we used polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) as a sacrificial layer to connect the silicon wafer with PDMS. Taking advantage of the solubility of PMMA in acetone, the sacrificial layer was removed by wet etching, resulting in the successful fabrication of a wafer-level flexible array film, which was then converted to a wafer-level carbon-based fluorescent array film by annealing. A detailed representation of the sample map and optical characterization map is shown in Fig. 8 . This research achievement not only opens new avenues for the flexible application research of carbon-based optical devices but also provides new possibilities and directions for future applications.

Considering the diversity, complexity, and demand for high-precision manufacturing of new display information, we adopted a high-resolution, high-precision, efficient, and highly controllable ultraviolet lithography process [58,59]. This process can integrate the manufacturing of complex and varied display information on a mask template, thereby achieving high-precision replication of this information through ultraviolet lithography technology. In this research work, we successfully prepared three types of patterns, including the English word 'Carbon,' the motto of North University in Chinese characters, and the emblem of North University. Subsequently, through a high-temperature annealing process, we manufactured carbon-based fluorescent display devices. As shown in Fig. 9 , we used a fluorescent inverted microscope to excite the samples under different wavelengths of light sources to verify their optical properties. The test results showed that the prepared samples not only had complete structures and high resolution but also exhibited good fluorescent effects. By simply changing the patterns on the mask template, our method can efficiently achieve the integrated manufacturing of information, effectively addressing the current challenges of diversity and complexity in display information. Moreover, this research provides new momentum for the development of carbon-based fluorescent optical devices in the field of novel fluorescent displays.

In the era of big data, there is an increasing demand for low-energy and long-term secure storage of massive data [60]. As an important solution for massive data storage, optical storage has attracted attention for its energy efficiency and security. However, due to the physical limitations of traditional optical storage technology, improving the storage density and capacity has become the focus of research. As shown in Fig. 10 (a), a 2D type carbon-based fluorescent code has been successfully fabricated by using the fabrication technique of “lithography + annealing”. Its storage cell size reaches 20 μm × 20 μm, enabling high-capacity information storage on a 4-inch storage substrate. The fluorescent coding material was irradiated with three wavelengths of light sources: 365 nm, 485 nm, and 525 nm. We observed the production of yellow, green, and red fluorescence and found that these colors disappear when the light source is turned off. This allows the 2D type code to achieve invisibility in visible light environments, demonstrating its excellent performance in light conditioning and information security storage. In addition, we also realized the preparation of 4-inch carbon-based fluorescent films, which further enhanced the information security storage capability. As shown in Fig. 10(b), when the 2D type code is visible, the information contained therein can be read by a smart phone, highlighting the great potential of carbon-based fluorescent materials in the field of information security storage.

In summary, carbon-based fluorescent materials have garnered significant interest for fluorescent displays and information security storage, owing to their unique display mechanisms and adjustable optical properties. Given that traditional storage technology is approaching its physical limits, developing storage media with higher density and capacity has become crucial. This study successfully developed a novel carbon-based fluorescent flexible film on a 4-inch wafer, comprising a PDMS substrate layer and a carbon-based fluorescent layer, using the lithography and annealing process. We achieved precise control over the 2D type code storage unit size, measuring 20 μm × 20 μm, and integrated it onto the wafer-level flexible film for high-capacity information storage. The carbon-based fluorescent film can display yellow, green, and red when exposed to varying wavelengths of excitation light. It exhibits high fluorescence intensity at the 603 nm band, with a fluorescence lifetime of 0.3517 ns. These unique optical properties not only bolster information storage security but also signal the material's broad application potential in optical data storage, advanced anti-counterfeiting, and information encryption.

Shengguo Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Kaixi Bi: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Liuyu Hou: Software, Data curation. Miaoli Guo: Visualization, Software. Yan Zhuang: Software, Investigation. Shuai Zhang: Software, Data curation. Qiannan Li: Validation, Supervision. Shuqi Han: Validation, Supervision. Longhao Liu: Software. Linyu Mei: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52275577 and Grant No. 52205609); Key Research and Development Project Key Program of Shanxi Province, China (Grant No. 202102040201007); General project of Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (Grant No. 20210302123056 and Grant No. 202203021223005); Shanxi Provincial Postgraduate Scientific Research Innovation Project (Grant No. 2023SJ195 and Grant No. 2023KY597); The 19th graduate science and technology project of North University of China (Grant No. 20231906).
